label: Knee abrasion without deep wound or infection red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit a minor knee abrasion.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a minor knee abrasion. We did not find a deep wound, retained foreign body, bite wound, infection, or another wound emergency today.
home: Keep the area clean and dry as your clinician instructed.
Change dressings, wash gently, and protect the area only as your clinician recommended.
Avoid soaking, picking, or reopening the abrasion.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Use antibiotic ointment, tetanus care, or other wound treatment only if your clinician recommended it.
return: Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, worsening swelling, or worsening pain.
Numbness, weakness, trouble moving the area, bleeding that will not stop, or concern that something is still in the wound.
The wound opens, worsens, or does not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, wound care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of minor knee abrasion.

No repair-needed laceration, deep structure injury, retained foreign body, bite wound, dirty wound requiring separate pathway, infection, diabetic-foot risk when relevant, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Repair-needed laceration, tendon/nerve/blood-vessel injury, joint violation, open fracture, retained foreign body, bite wound, dirty wound, infection concern, neurovascular compromise, severe pain, delayed high-risk presentation, diabetic foot when relevant, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed wound plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports basic cuts and wound-care self-care, keeping wounds clean, clinician-directed tetanus/ointment decisions, and escalation for infection, bleeding, retained foreign body, or deep-structure concern. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed minor abrasions with no deep structure, foreign-body, bite, dirty wound, infection, pregnancy/pediatric, or specialist-directed pathway.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of knee abrasion
no deep structure injury or retained foreign body

label: Ankle rubbing blister without diabetic-foot or infection red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit an uncomplicated friction blister on the ankle.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a friction blister on the ankle. We did not find burn, infection, poor blood flow, deep wound, or another skin emergency today.
home: Protect the blister from rubbing or pressure.
Keep the area clean and dry as your clinician instructed.
Do not cut or dig into the blister unless your clinician specifically instructed you.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Use creams, ointments, dressings, or padding only as your clinician recommended.
return: Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, or worsening swelling.
New numbness, blue or pale skin, severe pain, black skin, or rapidly worsening symptoms.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, wound care, podiatry if foot-related, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated ankle friction blister.
No burn, chemical exposure, infection, diabetic-foot risk when relevant, neurovascular compromise, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Burn or chemical wound, cellulitis or infection concern, open deep wound, diabetic foot when relevant, poor circulation, neuropathy, neurovascular compromise, severe pain, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed wound/podiatry plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports blister framing, protecting the area from friction, keeping the area clean, and escalation for infection or worsening symptoms. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated friction blisters with burn, infection, diabetic-foot risk when relevant, neurovascular, pregnancy/pediatric, and specialist-directed pathways absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of ankle rubbing blister
